1: The narrative opens with a list of four kings who form the imperial coalition: אַמְרָפֶל (Amrāpel, Amraphel) king of Shinar (= Babylonia, cf. 10:10; 11:2); אַרְיוֹךְ (ʾAryôḵ, Arioch) king of Ellasar; כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר (Kĕḏarlāʿomer, Kedorlaomer) king of Elam — an Elamite compound name meaning "servant of [the god] Lagamar"; תִּדְעָל (Tiḏʿāl, Tidal) king of nations (gôyim). The historical identification of these kings with specific ancient rulers is debated; the narrative does not depend on the identifications. What matters is the geopolitical scope: the eastern imperial coalition spans from Babylonia to Elam.
2: The five Canaanite kings of the plain include בֶּרַע (Beraʿ, Bera) king of Sodom and בִּרְשַׁע (Biršaʿ, Birsha) king of Gomorrah — both names may pun on Hebrew moral terms: beraʿ ("in evil") and biršaʿ ("in wickedness") — the narrator may be encoding the moral character of Sodom and Gomorrah's kings in their names, though this is debated as a wordplay vs. actual Semitic names. The city list (Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, Bela/Zoar) is the pentapolis of the plain, the same cities mentioned in 10:19 and whose destruction is narrated in 19:24-25.
3: עֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים הוּא יָם הַמֶּלַח — "the valley of Siddim, that is, the Salt Sea." The parenthetical identification equates the Valley of Siddim with the Dead Sea — the narrator writes from a post-destruction perspective, when the valley (once fertile, 13:10) has become the Salt Sea. The five city-plain is now underwater. This geographic parenthetical functions as a proleptic epitaph: even before the battle is described, the reader knows the valley ceased to exist.
4: שְׁתֵּים עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה עָבְדוּ אֶת-כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר — "twelve years they served Kedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled." The twelve-year submission and thirteenth-year rebellion provides the political context: the Canaanite pentapolis had been vassals of the Elamite king. The fourteenth year brings the punitive campaign. The numbers (12/13/14) track the years precisely — unusual specificity that signals either archival source material or deliberate literary framing of the event as a genuine historical incident within the patriarchal period.
5: הָרְפָאִים (hārĕpāʾîm, the Rephaim) — "the ancient ones/mighty ones"; these pre-Israelite giants appear throughout the OT (Deut 2:10-11, 20-21; 3:11; Josh 12:4; 13:12; 17:15; 1 Chron 20:4-8). The Rephaim were associated with both ancient pre-Canaanite peoples and with the shades of the dead in Sheol (Isa 14:9; 26:14; Ps 88:10; Job 26:5). The campaign sweeps through the entire Transjordanian territory, clearing out ancient peoples — the coalition's military reach is comprehensive and the narrative emphasizes the scope of their conquest before Abram enters the story.
6: הַחֹרִי בְּהַרֵרָם שֵׂעִיר — "the Horites in their mountain territory of Seir." The Horites (ḥōrî, cave-dwellers?) are the pre-Edomite inhabitants of the Seir region (Deut 2:12, 22), displaced by Esau's descendants. The campaign route sweeps south through the Transjordan, then curves back north — a military encirclement strategy that allows the eastern coalition to strike the southern Canaanite cities from the rear after isolating them geographically.
7: עֵין מִשְׁפָּט הִוא קָדֵשׁ — "En-mishpat, that is, Kadesh." The parenthetical identification links the obscure battle-site name with the well-known Kadesh-barnea of the wilderness narratives (Num 13:26; 20:1; Deut 1:2) — where Israel camped before the conquest. The coalition's route presages the later route of the Exodus-Conquest. אֵת כָּל-שְׂדֵה הָעֲמָלֵקִי — "the whole territory of the Amalekites" — Amalek appears as a people long before Esau's grandson Amalek of 36:12; the name here may be ethnic rather than eponymous, identifying the pre-Israelite inhabitants of the Negev.
8: The five Canaanite kings march out to meet the eastern coalition in battle in the Valley of Siddim. The narrative structure is deliberate: after tracing the eastern coalition's comprehensive sweep through the Transjordan (vv. 5-7), the five kings emerge to fight — the setup makes clear that the Canaanite armies have been watching a vastly superior force that has already routed every regional power in its path. The battle is practically decided before it begins.
9: The battle is framed numerically: four kings against five. Despite outnumbering the coalition, the five Canaanite kings lose. The numerical contrast (four vs. five, which should favor the Canaanites) heightens the drama of the defeat and sets up Abram's counterintuitive victory with only 318 men (v. 14). The pattern of the few defeating the many — a motif running from Gideon (Judg 7) to David (1 Sam 17) — is anticipated here in embryonic form.
10: בְּאֵרֹת חֵמָר עֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים — "the Valley of Siddim was full of tar pits." Ḥēmār (bitumen, tar, asphalt) — the same ḥēmār used as the mortar of Babel's tower (11:3) and for Moses's basket (Exod 2:3). The bitumen pits of the Dead Sea region are archaeologically attested; the valley's terrain was treacherous for retreating armies. The fleeing kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fall into the pits — a military disaster that explains how Lot and Sodom's goods are taken by the coalition without further resistance.
11: וַיִּקְחוּ אֶת-כָּל-רְכֻשׁ סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה — "they took all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah and all their food." Reḵûš (goods, possessions, property) — the term that will describe Abram's own wealth (12:5; 15:14); the coalition takes exactly what Lot had been accumulating by living near Sodom. The taking of food (ʾoḵel) alongside goods represents total spoliation — stripping the cities of everything.
12: וַיִּקְחוּ אֶת-לוֹט וְאֶת-רְכֻשׁוֹ — "they took Lot and his possessions." Lot, who had chosen the well-watered plain for its wealth, has his wealth taken away along with his person. The irony tracks precisely: the choice of material abundance (13:10-11) leads directly to captivity. בֶּן-אֲחִי אַבְרָם (son of Abram's brother) — a reminder of the family connection that will motivate Abram's rescue; the covenant line's kinship-loyalty supersedes the political situation.
13: אַבְרָם הָעִבְרִי (ʾaḇrām hāʿiḇrî) — "Abram the Hebrew." Haʿiḇrî is the first use of this designator for Abram — and, notably, it is given by outsiders. ʿIḇrî (Hebrew) derives from ʿāḇar (to cross over, to pass through) and may mean "the one from the other side" (across the Euphrates — consistent with Josh 24:2-3: "your fathers lived beyond the River"). It is an ethnic-territorial label, not a self-designation — Abram is identified as the stranger-from-beyond-the-river. Later, the term ʿiḇrî will be used almost exclusively in contexts where Israelites are identified to non-Israelites (Exod 1:15-16; 2:6, 11; Jon 1:9; Gen 39:14, 17; 40:15; 41:12).
14: חֲנִיכָיו יְלִידֵי בֵיתוֹ שְׁמֹנָה עָשָׂר וּשְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת — "his trained men, born in his household, 318." Ḥănîḵîm (trained men, retainers) — from ḥānaḵ (to train, dedicate, initiate), the root of ḥănukkāh (dedication). These are household-born professional fighting men, trained warriors rather than mercenaries. 318 men against the coalition that had just swept the entire Transjordan — the disparity is stark and sets up the miraculous character of the victory. Abram "pursues as far as Dan" — Dan here is the northern limit of later Israelite territory, a geographical note that may use the place-name anachronistically (the city is renamed Dan in Judg 18:29).
15: וַיַּחֲלֵק עֲלֵיהֶם לַיְלָה — "he divided his forces against them by night." The night-attack strategy (dividing forces, surprise, darkness) echoes military tactics attested in ancient Near Eastern warfare. Wayyaḥăleq (Piel of ḥālaq, to divide, allot) — the same root as the division of the land (Josh 14:5) and Babel's division. Abram pursues "to Hobah, which is north of Damascus" — the pursuit extends far beyond Canaan's borders into Syria, indicating either exceptional military success or divine enablement not explicitly narrated.
16: וַיָּשֶׁב אֵת כָּל-הָרְכוּשׁ — "he recovered all the goods." The keyword reḵûš (goods/possessions) is recovered completely — the total spoil the coalition had taken (v. 11) is fully restored. וְגַם אֶת-לוֹט אָחִיו וּרְכֻשׁוֹ הֵשִׁיב — "and also his relative Lot and his possessions he brought back." The military victory serves the covenant purpose: Lot is rescued so that the family of the promise is preserved. The restoration is complete: people, goods, and women — all that the coalition had taken.
17: עֵמֶק שָׁוֵה הוּא עֵמֶק הַמֶּלֶךְ — "the Valley of Shaveh, that is, the King's Valley." Šāweh = level/plain; the parenthetical identifies it with the King's Valley, which later tradition (2 Sam 18:18) locates near Jerusalem — where Absalom erected his pillar. The meeting of Abram with the king of Sodom and with Melchizedek in the same valley creates a dramatic contrast: the Sodom king offers goods (vv. 21-24); the king-priest of Salem offers bread, wine, and blessing (vv. 18-20).
18: מַלְכִּי-צֶדֶק מֶלֶךְ שָׁלֵם (Malkî-ṣeḏeq melek šālēm) — "Melchizedek, king of Salem." The name Malkî-ṣeḏeq = "my king is righteous" or "king of righteousness" — the same components reappear in Ps 110:4 (the Davidic king declared "a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek") and in Hebrews 7 as the key to the new covenant priesthood. Šālēm = "peace/wholeness" — most plausibly Jerusalem (Ps 76:2: "in Salem is his tent") before it received the name Jebus or Jerusalem. כֹּהֵן לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן (kōhēn lĕʾēl ʿelyôn) — "priest of God Most High." Melchizedek holds the dual office of king and priest simultaneously — an office that Israelite law explicitly separated (Uzziah is struck with leprosy for trying it, 2 Chr 26:16-21) but that will be united in the Davidic king (Ps 110:4) and fulfilled in Christ.
19: בָּרוּךְ אַבְרָם לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ — "blessed be Abram by God Most High, maker/possessor of heaven and earth." Qôneh (Qal participle of qānāh) — a word with two senses: to acquire/possess, and to create (Gen 14:22; Prov 8:22; Deut 32:6). Applied to God with "heaven and earth," both meanings converge: God is both Creator and Possessor of all things. The epithet ʾēl ʿelyôn (God Most High) is the Canaanite/Semitic title Melchizedek uses — which Abram will identify with YHWH in v. 22, equating the pre-Israelite divine name with the covenant name.
20: וּבָרוּךְ אֵל עֶלְיוֹן אֲשֶׁר-מִגֵּן צָרֶיךָ בְיָדֶךָ — "and blessed be God Most High, who delivered your enemies into your hand." Migen (Piel of māgan, to deliver, hand over) — the same root as māgēn (shield) in 15:1 ("I am your shield"). ʾĂšer-miggen ṣārêḵā bĕyāḏĕḵā — the deliverance formula that will echo through the Exodus narrative: "I have given [Pharaoh/the kings/the land] into your hand." וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ מַעֲשֵׂר מִכֹּל — "he gave him a tenth of everything." The first tithe in Scripture — Abram voluntarily gives a tenth of the battle spoils to the priest-king. Hebrews 7:9-10 argues that Levi "paid the tithe through Abraham" — the ancestor's tithe to a superior priest establishes a precedent for Melchizedek's priesthood being greater than the Levitical order.
21: תֶּן-לִי הַנֶּפֶשׁ וְהָרְכֻשׁ קַח-לָךְ — "give me the persons and take the goods for yourself." The king of Sodom makes a practical offer: he wants his people back; Abram can keep the goods as a military reward. Nepeš here = persons/lives (the people of Sodom carried off by the coalition). The offer is customary by ancient war-spoils convention — but Abram's response will refuse both people and goods to maintain his witness before the LORD.
22: הֲרִמֹתִי יָדִי אֶל-יְהוָה אֵל עֶלְיוֹן קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ — "I have raised my hand to the LORD, God Most High, maker of heaven and earth." Hărîmōtî yāḏî (I have raised my hand) — the oath gesture: lifting the hand toward heaven when swearing (cf. Deut 32:40; Ezek 20:5, 6; Dan 12:7; Rev 10:5-6). Crucially, Abram identifies YHWH with the ʾēl ʿelyôn Melchizedek had named: he equates the Canaanite title Melchizedek used with the covenant name YHWH. This identification is the theological heart of the Melchizedek encounter — the Most High God worshiped by this pre-Israelite priest is the same God who called Abram from Ur.
23: אִם-מִחוּט וְעַד שְׂרוֹךְ-נַעַל — "if from a thread to a sandal strap." The merism of smallest (thread) to least valuable-but-functional (sandal strap) expresses total refusal: Abram will take nothing from Sodom's king. The reason follows: לֹא-אַתָּה תֹאמַר הֶעֱשַׁרְתִּי אֶת-אַבְרָם — "so that you cannot say, 'I made Abram rich.'" Abram's wealth must come from YHWH alone, not from the king of the wicked city. The honor-system logic is precise: accepting Sodom's goods would give Sodom's king a patron-claim over Abram, crediting him rather than God for Abram's prosperity.
24: בִּלְעָדַי רַק אֲשֶׁר אָכְלוּ הַנְּעָרִים — "except what the young men have eaten." Abram's refusal is complete for himself but not imposed on his allies: Aner, Eshkol, and Mamre (the Amorite confederates of 13:18; 14:13) are entitled to their share of the spoil by the norms of the war-spoils custom. Abram distinguishes between his personal vow (nišbaʿtî, I have sworn) and the rightful claims of his allies. The moral restraint is complete and carefully bounded — not an ascetic rejection of all goods, but a specific refusal tied to a specific oath to YHWH.
